dynamic.architecture_summary: Single-process Flask application following a simple two-layer architecture: route handlers in src/app.py and domain models plus an in-memory store in src/models.py. The OrderStore class acts as a repository abstraction over a dict, designed to be swapped for a real database. There is no persistence layer, ORM, or external service dependency.

key_components.name: app
key_components.responsibility: Flask application defining all REST API route handlers (health, create, get, list, cancel orders).

key_components.name: models
key_components.responsibility: Domain models (Order dataclass) and in-memory OrderStore repository.

key_components.name: test_orders
key_components.responsibility: Test suite covering the order API endpoints.

agent_safe_operations: Adding new model classes or fields in src/models.py
Adding new test files under tests/
Adding new Python modules under src/
Updating requirements.txt or requirements-dev.txt with new dependencies
Running pytest tests/ -v to verify changes
agent_forbidden_operations: Removing or renaming existing API endpoints without explicit instruction
Introducing breaking changes to the Order.to_dict() serialization format
Replacing the in-memory store with a database without explicit instruction
potential_pitfalls: The app module uses relative imports (from .models import ...), so it must be run as a package (python -m flask --app src/app run), not directly with python src/app.py.
OrderStore is instantiated at module level in src/app.py — state resets on every process restart; tests must account for shared in-memory state or use Flask's test client.
The cancel endpoint does not guard against non-cancellable statuses beyond 'cancelled' (e.g., a 'shipped' status would still be cancellable).
No CORS, authentication, or rate-limiting middleware is configured — adding these may affect existing test expectations.
Flask abort() returns HTML error pages by default, not JSON — test assertions on error responses need to handle this.
